title: Emulation of Optically Interconnected Quantum Data Centers Topologies for Cost-Fidelity Benchmarking
authors: Seyed Navid Elyasi, Seyed Morteza Ahmadian, Rui Lin, Paolo Monti
abstract: We emulate optically interconnected quantum processors in ring, star, and line topologies using a quantum computer. GHZ benchmarks show that the star provides the best trade-off between cost and fidelity under transduction and fiber noise.
summary: This paper uses a digital quantum computer to simulate different network architectures for quantum data centers. It finds that a star topology is the most efficient for balancing hardware cost with high-fidelity entanglement distribution. The study specifically highlights that transduction noise is the most significant hurdle for large-scale quantum interconnects.
main_problem: Evaluating the trade-off between interconnection cost and fidelity in different Quantum Data Center (QDC) topologies, specifically addressing how transduction and fiber noise impact distributed quantum computing.
main_result: The star topology provides the optimal trade-off between cost and fidelity, outperforming ring and line topologies by minimizing noise accumulation and SWAP operations. Transduction noise was identified as the primary driver of fidelity degradation.
method: The study uses a digital quantum computer (IBM Quantum Torino) to emulate optically interconnected QPUs using a three-layer simulation framework (circuit, configuration, and experimental layers) and a collisional model for noise.
model_or_system: The system consists of interconnected QPUs in ring, star, and line topologies, each containing processing, communication, and flying qubits. The noise is modeled via a collisional model with an interaction Hamiltonian between communication qubits and an environment qubit to simulate transduction and fiber loss.
key_observables: GHZ-state fidelity, RCNOT gate performance, and communication cost (number of entanglement links).
important_parameters: Transduction coupling strength (kappa = 0.5), fiber attenuation constant (0.0392 km^-1), and topology-specific link counts.
main_assumptions: The noise is modeled using a Markovian evolution approach where the environment qubit is reset after each iteration.
figures_summary: Fig. 1 illustrates the QDC topologies, the simulation framework, and the collisional noise model; Fig. 2 details the GHZ-state circuit and the cat-comm protocol for RCNOT.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QDC scalability problem, describes the physical model and noise implementation, details the emulation framework on IBM hardware, presents the comparative results of different topologies, and concludes with a cost-fidelity analysis.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it quantifies how environmental coupling and photon loss impact the scalability of distributed quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a)–(c) Ring, line, and star QDC topologies. (d) Entanglement distribution via SWAP oper- ations. (e) Simulation layers: circuit, configuration, and experimental layers. (f) Collisional noise model for transduction and fiber effects.
Fig. 2. (a) GHZ-state circuit connecting processing qubits for QDC evaluation. (b) Cat-comm pro- tocol implementing the remote CNOT gate with noisy entanglement generation.
Fig. 3. (a) Communication cost for GHZ-state execution across QDC topologies. (b) RCNOT fi- delity results based on the noise model. (c) Fidelity results for the GHZ circuit.
